summary: I went to school in Nashville, specifically at Hume-Fogg High School. That was back in the early 1920s, around 1922 or 1923 when I finished high school. Nashville was where my early education took place before I moved on to college and seminary.

I attended Hume-Fogg High School in Nashville and graduated around 1922 or 1923. It was my stepping stone before I went on to Maryville College. My time there marked the beginning of my educational journey.

I went to Maryville College in Tennessee around 1920 or 1925 after high school. It was a pretty strict and conservative place with rigid rules about dormitory hours and study times. Intellectually, it didn't change my outlook much, but it was an important part of my early adult life.

I spent some time in Arkansas selling with my dad before I went to college. That was part of my early life, around the early 1920s, before I decided to leave home and pursue further education. It was a time when I was figuring out what I wanted to do.

I went to St. Louis for a while because I just wanted to leave home and experience something new. This was around 1920, shortly after finishing high school. It was part of my journey before I settled on going to seminary.

In 1929, I came to Richmond, Virginia, to attend seminary. I stayed there until the spring of 1932, and it was a big change for me socially, even if not so much intellectually. Richmond was where I really started my path toward becoming a Presbyterian preacher.

Before seminary, I had a cafe in San Antonio, Texas, which my dad and I were involved with. My dad was always eager to travel and buy new businesses, including a hotel in Cotulla, Texas. This was in the 1920s, a time when I was still figuring out my direction in life.

My dad got excited about a hotel in Cotulla, Texas, and that was part of our family's ventures in the 1920s. I was involved with a cafe and hotel business during that time before I decided to pursue seminary. Cotulla was one of the stops on our travels and business efforts.

I didn't meet Buck Kester until after I went to Richmond Seminary. He came up to Burnsville once, and that was after my first year at Richmond. We knew some of the same people, but our paths crossed later in my ministry journey.

Burnsville was a place where Buck Kester came up once after I had been at Richmond Seminary. It was part of the fellowship and connections I made during my early ministry years. Burnsville is linked to some of the people I met after seminary.

As I mentioned, Maryville College was where I went to college in the early 1920s. It was a conservative place with strict dormitory rules. My intellectual outlook didn't change much there, but it was an important step before seminary and ministry.

A Presbyterian man named Mr. Bates in San Antonio encouraged me to go to Austin for music education, but I didn't want to because it was too far from home. Instead, I chose to go back closer to home and eventually ended up at Richmond Seminary. Austin was an option I considered but declined.

After seminary, I went to Clarkesville, Virginia, where I worked with a home mission secretary named Garrison. I did summer Vacation Bible School work there and eventually got two churches to serve. Clarkesville was my first pastoral experience after seminary in the early 1930s.

I've been a Presbyterian all my life on my mother's side, and my entire ministry has been within the Presbyterian Church. From my early days at Maryville to seminary in Richmond and then various churches in Virginia and North Carolina, the Presbyterian Church has been central to my vocation. I served congregations and was involved in some denominational conflicts later on.

During the summer after seminary, I worked with Vacation Bible School in Clarkesville, Virginia. It was part of my early ministry experience under the guidance of a home mission secretary named Garrison. That work helped me gain pastoral responsibilities and eventually led to church assignments.

After starting with two churches in Clarkesville, I expanded my pastoral duties to include Madison, Virginia. This was part of my early ministry in the 1930s before moving on to Brevard College. Madison was one of the communities I served as a young minister.

I served at Brevard College in North Carolina for about five years in the 1930s. Dr. Ernest Trice Thompson, a professor of history there, was a special friend and gave me lectures he also gave at Harvard. Brevard was an important chapter in my ministry and academic life.

Dr. Ernest Trice Thompson was a professor of history at Brevard College and a special friend of mine. He gave me the same series of lectures he gave at Harvard, which was a significant intellectual influence during my time at Brevard in the 1930s. I valued his friendship and mentorship greatly.

Though I never attended Harvard myself, Dr. Ernest Trice Thompson at Brevard College gave me the same lectures he delivered at Harvard. This connection brought some Harvard-level academic insight into my education during the 1930s. It was a unique and enriching experience.

I came to Chapel Hill in 1941, just before World War II started, and have lived here ever since except for one year away during church difficulties. I served at the First Presbyterian Church here, which later became University Presbyterian Church. Chapel Hill has been my home for most of my ministry career.

I arrived in Chapel Hill in 1941, just as World War II was beginning. The war years marked a significant period during my ministry at the First Presbyterian Church. Despite the global conflict, I continued to serve the church and community through those challenging times.

entity: First Presbyterian Church (Chapel Hill, North Carolina)
summary: I served as pastor at the First Presbyterian Church in Chapel Hill, which was part of the Southern Presbyterian Church. It was a thriving church with two Sunday services, located right on Franklin Street across from the campus. My time there included both growth and conflict, especially around 1952 when the Presbytery took control away from the local church.

The First Presbyterian Church in Chapel Hill where I served was part of the Southern Presbyterian Church. This denomination was distinct from the Northern Presbyterian Church, with the Mason-Dixon line marking the division. The Southern Church was the context for much of my ministry and the conflicts I faced in the 1950s.

entity: University Presbyterian Church (Chapel Hill, North Carolina)
summary: The church I served in Chapel Hill eventually came to be called University Presbyterian Church. It was located on Franklin Street, very close to the university dormitories, which helped attract many students. This proximity made it a lively and sizable congregation during my tenure.

The church I pastored was located on Franklin Street in Chapel Hill, right across from the university dormitories. This location was key because students could easily walk to church, which helped maintain a large and active congregation. Franklin Street was central to the church's identity and outreach.

T. Henry Patterson was a church executive in the North Carolina Presbytery who played a significant role in the conflict I experienced in the early 1950s. He was instrumental in initiating the Presbytery's takeover of my church's governance in 1952. Patterson represented the conservative forces opposing some of my more liberal stances.

The Mason-Dixon line marked the division between the Northern and Southern Presbyterian Churches. Tennessee was unique in having congregations from both sides, reflecting divided sympathies. This division was deeply tied to the Civil War and affected church affiliations in my ministry context.

The University of Virginia library holds a thesis written by a Presbyterian minister about the church conflict I was involved in during the 1950s. I never got around to reviewing it fully, but it covers the situation in detail. The university is a resource for understanding that difficult chapter in my ministry.

I attended seminary in Richmond, Virginia, from 1929 to 1932, which was a formative period in my life. Though the University of Richmond itself wasn't specifically mentioned, the seminary experience in Richmond shaped my ministry. Richmond was where I gained social freedom and began my pastoral career.

I stopped using the Apostles' Creed in church because many students didn't know it and wouldn't affirm something they didn't believe. This was part of the liberal approach I took in my ministry, which eventually caused conflict with the Presbytery. The Creed became a symbol of the theological disputes I faced in the 1950s.
